1) I’ve decided to take action to address the fact my life truly sucks right now. I’ve identified two addictions I have which are getting in the way of this project though: porn and the internet in general. I’m mulling over what an addiction is, and have come to the conclusion it is a divided will. This seems to suggest that if I can unify my will I can eliminate my addictions. Is this a reasonable chain of logic? How do you go about unifying the will?

2) I’m planning to address my addictions directly through journaling and slowly whittling away at my internet addiction by identifying things I do online but could do without the internet and moving them one by one away from it; as well as the less direct way of pursuing a systematic transformation of my life. I think the internet and porn addictions are both a cause and symptom of the fact my life sucks, and so if I can give myself a life worth living, I’ll be able to address my addictions easier. Are there other steps you can think of I can take?

3) As part of this process I plan to start working through some ritual magic, starting with LRM and then moving on to Circles of Power/Paths of Wisdom, The Tree of Life, and Dion Fortune’s writings. (I plan to spend the rest of this incarnation on the task; and I’m young and long lives run in my family, so I expect to have plenty of time for it!) I’m thus planning to take the time while I work through LRM to look at the three other books, so that I can see what is worth learning there as well. Is this a reasonable thing to do, or would I be better off using the three books I know I’ll want to work through later?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
All this is very good to hear.

1) Yes, it's a reasonable chain of logic, but the unification of the will is not something you can do easily or all at once. You will spend the rest of this incarnation working on it. Mind you, even your first baby steps will transform your life! I'll be doing a series of posts on training the will here on Dreamwidth shortly, which should give you some help -- but the simple routine of taking up an occult practice and maintaining it will do a great deal to help you get started.

2) That is to say, you've recognized the vicious circle: addiction --> misery --> more addiction --> more misery, and so on. That's crucial. Taking it apart a step at a time is the best way to go about it.

3) By all means read the three further books while you're working on LRM. The path of self-initiation requires a lot of self-correction, and having a good sense of the options is useful in that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Division of the will is the single most common cause of failure in human life. It's also all but universal among human beings -- it's one of the central challenges inherent in the human condition, and overcoming it usually happens right before we pass to stages above the human level. That is to say, having realized the division of your will and decided to do something about it, you're on the way to magnificent things.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Returning to the ever-popular topic of pornography, my thinking on the topic has led me to the conclusion that it's an inherently frustrating genre, because it can never deliver a genuine sexual experience, which is what its audience craves the most. It's like watching a fast food ad that triggers your salivary glands, and pretending that you had a decent meal while drooling all over yourself. Porn triggers a physiological response that roughly approximates what happens during the real thing, but is otherwise completely artificial, stripped of its context, the sizzle without the steak.

I've also realized that it has negative psychological effects on men, particularly in how they see and interact with women, one of them being a sense of entitlement to women's bodies, and the delusion that every woman is sexually willing and available at any time. I see many of the male pathologies of our time, from PUAs to the incels, as being subliminally influenced by the pornographic mentality. While I don't think there's anything wrong with sexualizing the other gender in some contexts (perpetuation of the species and all that), the oversexualization of women prevents men from interacting with them as human beings.

So my question is: do you think the above conclusions have any merit? And secondly, could masturbation, even without porn, have similar effects if carried out in a "porn-like" state of mind?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes and yes. If you've saturated your imagination with pornographic imagery, what you imagine while masturbating will be little more than a rehash of the last hundred or so pieces of pornography you've seen. That's true of all visual media, btw -- your thoughts about products, for example, will be shaped by the last thousand or so TV ads you've seen. (This is one of the many reasons why getting rid of your television is so useful a step in personal liberation.)

Keep in mind, btw, that cheap romance fills the same role in women's mentalities that cheap pornography fills in men's mentalities. It encourages emotional rather than sexual self-stimulation, but it has negative psychological effects on women, shaping how they see and interact with men in equally dysfunctional ways.

John,

replies.title: Re: Incence
replies.content: There's a lot of very crappy incense out there. Goldenhawk

comments.title: Effects of being the target of sexual fantasies?

I'm male, but have never been addicted to pornography myself.

I found myself being more susceptible to sexual fantasies and memories when masturbating, I tried giving up masturbation a few times before, my longest stretch lasted more than 3 months before I met a partner, after that I masturbated to sexual memories and thoughts of her. replies.title: Re: Effects of being the target of sexual fantasies?
replies.content: The target -- the person you're imagining -- is likely to feel a brief period of sexual arousal directed at you. If the two of you are in a relationship, that's not a problem; if the two of you broke up, she's going to think of you, and then all the reasons behind the breakup are going to come to mind, and she'll react against the arousal by feeling even less attraction to you than before.

replies.title: Re: Effects of being the target of sexual fantasies?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Funny. Yes, "prehend" is one good translation of "grok," and if you like to grok with tentacles, you'll get along well with the characters in the Haliverse.

As for the Shambler, indirectly, yes. The direct source is a very early Robert Bloch story, "The Shambler from the Stars," which was influenced by Doyle's tale. Bloch was a teenage kid when he wrote "Shambler;" he was a fan and correspondent of H.P. Lovecraft's, and asked his permission to write a story in which Lovecraft was devoured by an invisible monster. Lovecraft was delighted by the prospect -- and then returned the favor in "The Haunter of the Dark," in which a character very obviously based on Bloch suffered an equally hideous fate.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

When I started magical practice a couple of years ago, my desire to make music and play music dropped off. With a full-time job, I can't do both. After journalling and self-reflection, I realized that a major motivation behind making and playing music was for the approval of others, mixed in with a genuine joy of the music itself. I'd often imagine playing music at an instrument in front of other people, and receiving their awe and appreciation at my skill. I understand that with any art, the desire to share it with others is a healthy motivation, but in me it is particularly ego-driven as it seems I desired self-validation from others.

In reality though, although I was fast learner, I can't play with any great skill, I started learning an instrument way too late in life, my fingers just aren't that dextrous as I age, and during the few times I played in public, I was far too awkward to get anywhere close to the fantasies I described above.

With magic, it's so much different. I seemed to take to it like a duck to water. The practice of playing a music instrument daily has given me the self-discipline to do divination, the SOP, and meditate daily. My past background in art school, and an overactive imagination when I was a kid, has helped give me some useful skills clearly helpful for magical practice. And no one except my wife knows I do magic at all, so it's not about feeding my ego. It appears that I am drawn to magic in itself, and especially, for a lack of a better term, the 'quest for wisdom'. It looks like it has always been a theme I was not cognisant of until the last few years.

However music has been a goal for so long that I still struggle with letting it go. I can't tell if I am encountering the Watcher at the Threshold when it comes to music, and whether the relative ease I took to magic is something to be suspicious of, or embraced because perhaps I've found what I'm supposed to be doing.

I know this is a very personal thing I'm dealing with, just wondering if you might have any advice on the matter.

Thanks very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I feel you on this one as I am a musician (who has a lifelong career as a music teacher) who has been tempted to give it all up several times over my lifespan of nearly 50 years. Music is just so frustrating and yes, our success is often equated with how popular our music is or whether or not we are able to support ourselves. I ran into a music professor once who arrogantly crowed about not being a "real" musician unless you were able to support yourself making music like he did. So I guess by his rules P. Diddy was a more authentic musician than W.A. Mozart because $$$.

Like you, practice of my instruments prepared me for magic in a way I did not expect. I started the Druidry routine 2.5 years ago and though I'm loathe to say it out loud, I think I may be a bit of a gifted student. Well, at least I get a lot out of it, at any rate. And if you ever want to go tit for tat with overactive imagination syndrome, seriously, I think you may have met your match.

I don't know how long you've been doing your practices, but I noticed especially at the 1.5 year mark that my past lives started making themselves heard. I was a musician in a couple of past lives, one time as my career when I was also a Don Juan and ended up hanging from a scaffold for it, and other times just as a hobby. From what I can tell and thanks to what some of the gods have been willing to share with me, any musical skill you gain will be with you throughout your lifetimes, so you might as well learn and practice all you can in whatever time you can cram into your schedule. Musical skill is cumulative. This has kept me sane as a music teacher because no matter how badly my students denigrate themselves (piano is an especially demoralizing instrument BTW) I always have the serene knowledge that the more I lift them up, the more they will learn and what they do pick up will NEVER go away. Talk about a value for the money one spends on music lessons!

I sing the and play the accompaniments and melodies I have made for the Orphic hymns every night to approximately 3 people as a YouTube livestream, one of whom is my husband watching from the upper floor of our house. Almost nobody cares about the music I compose. I will die in obscurity. I'm absolutely fine with that.

As Oswald Spengler pointed out in The Decline of the West, high cultures normally abandon their traditional religion at a certain point in their history, at the beginning of their Age of Reason; when the Age of Reason winds down, a modified version of the traditional religion gets reestablished, though it lacks the intensity and power it had during the culture's early years; eventually, as the society's decline picks up speed, new religions that still have intensity and power come onto the scene and replace the old faith.

We're at a slightly earlier point in the cycle than Rome was in the 4th century. Western civilization is at the end of its Age of Reason, and so we can expect a revival of Christian observance in the near future. If it follows the usual pattern, it'll be embraced as much as a symbol of cultural identity as anything else, and it will take on the same moderate, calm, autumnal quality that classical religion had in its last few centuries. The new religions that will replace it are still some distance off, and may not even have been founded yet.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Last week, there was question about the safety of using the Emotional Freedom technique (EFT), and your reply was that you didn’t have any personal experience of it.
As it happens, it’s a technique I’ve used for decades, on myself and others, so I’m going to take the liberty of repeating the question with a few more details, in case you feel able to offer a judgement.
EFT seems to have begun with the recognition that in tradition Chinese medicine, each organ is associated with an emotion, and that each organ has its own meridian, or neural pathway in the body. So the first practitioners stimulated pressure points at the end of meridians to relieve emotional symptoms and, subsequently, physical symptoms as well. Eventually, this developed into a protocol of tapping on a series of 10-15 points in a given order. There are lots of variants but most systems start by tapping the side of the hand and repeating a phrase which expresses the problem (« I don’t feel motivated with my magical practices ») and noting roughly how intense the feeling is (conventionally on a scale of 1 to 10). Next, you tap on the same place repeating a concessive formula such as « even though I don’t feel motivated .. » and adding a positive statement such as « I accept myself anyway. » Then you go through the points using a shortened form of the statement « I don’t feel motivated.. » a few times. There’s no particular rationale to this procedure: it just seems to work after a lot of trial and error.
I’ve used this successfully for emotions, illnesses and diagnostically to see which statements about past lives produced the strongest results. At that level, I haven’t noticed any obvious problems. However, other practitioners have expanded the idea considerably, using it for pets, for other people (with or without their knowledge) and even for inanimate objects such as electronic equipment that won’t work, or money that they want. This latter seems a bit more questionable.
Any thoughts?

comments.title: Invisibility
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I remember you made an offhand reference to a Golden Dawn invisibility spell on MM a few weeks ago. And then last week I think you said something about putting on a Horus godform so that people got out of your way on the street - instinctively to me those two sound like mirror images of each other, although I don't know details.

Coincidentally, I read an essay by a well known self-defence expert recently (former street fighter and gang member, turned bouncer and law enforcer - basically someone who has spent his adult life in violence) and he talks about how he uses certain "tricks" or "abilities" (which he says he doesn't really understand and can't explain in any scientific way) to effectively become "invisible" - he says it's not as if he actually vanishes but people just stop noticing or registering him when he "switches on". He says a lot of professionals - spies, assassins, that sort of person - can do this, and when another professional "locks on" and notices them in a crowd, it instantly alerts the person who thought he was "invisible" and the "invisible" guy snaps up and looks for the person who has noticed him and so on.

He says there are 2-3 ways of doing it (he does it on a kind of instinctive level so I don't know how good the words are as a description):

1. Some form of making a lot of noise/being noticeable, and then suddenly stopping all of that and going into "stealth mode" - the contrast means people stop noticing.

2. Something based on a 1972 movie called Now You See Him Now You Don't where the protagonist learns to bend light around himself and reassemble it behind himself to become invisible. In this case, the guy doesn't bend light - he somehow "bends" the attention of people around him, and makes it go around himself instead of touching him (this can be countered by someone who knows what to look for). He says it's kind of like heat waves coming off the desert that cause a ripple in consciousness - you can see what's behind it but it is distorted. The trick is to shift your consciousness and look through the ripple to see what is there - otherwise known as "looking for the absence".

3. A way to disappear based on blending in and taking on the essential characteristics and vibe of your background or the item you're next to. So if you're sitting next to a chair, you just become "one" with the chair or the tree next to you or whatever - most people notice as an area of uninterest because the only vibe they get from that area is "chair" or "tree". Their brain just tells them they have no reason to notice anything there. But again this can be countered - someone with experience will "ping" there is something hidden there - if something is hidden and not noticed but the an experienced person gets a "predator" vibe where there shouldn't be on - that in itself will trigger a closer look at the tree and break through the cloak.

4. Similar to the above - you "pull into yourself so deeply that you become a blank spot" - the writer describes a crowd flowing along and a "spot of silence" he was able to sense flowing through the crowd - if he focussed on it and shifted his consciousness he could notice a small guy who was invisible to to the people he was moving around and who noticed immediately that someone (the writer) had "made" him.

I just wrote all that out because I find it interesting, but my question is: does the above make sense? Is it connected to "taking on a godform" or the Golden Dawn spell for invisibility in terms of similar tactics? And can "invisibility" be learnt as a skill by itself (ie, without taking on a full on ritual magic practice)?

replies.title: Re: Invisibility
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. The GD method for doing it is simply a convenient way to learn how, if you happen to be trained in ceremonial magic -- and the GD paper on the subject says that with practice, you can learn to do it without needing to use the ritual. The ninja had similar practices, which accounted for their ability to avoid notice. All of these are ways of using the same principle.

replies.title: Re: Invisibility
replies.content: I can verify that methods 3 and 4 work-- I have always been able to do that, not always on purpose, and have literally had people *who were looking for me* walk right by TWICE, while I was sitting on the couch two feet away, not camouflaged or anything. It gives a whole new flavor to being "part of the furniture".

replies.title: Wanting to be seen
replies.content:
Those are all very good techniques, thanks. The "Grey Man" skills encompass those, mostly not standing out and to blend in.

I've had people come into rooms and be literally unaware of my presence until I spoke to them, to their surprise. To me, everyone seems to want to be noticed, to the degree they almost silently scream, look at me, look at me! I just turn that off, consciously thinking to myself "Nothing here to see, nothing here to see".

It seems to be tied into your own eyes and the intent you put on to watch/see another person. The more focused you are on that other person, the more they feel your observation. Adopting a peaceful disregard of their presence, and absence of attention.

An article on zebras and their stripes said stripes aren't there to make an individual blend into the environment, but to blend into the herd. Lions literally can't see one zebra out of the many. the researchers put orange dye on a couple of zebras for study, to track them, and the lions then started stalking the dyed zebras, being able to now watch one zebra alone out of the background herd.

comments.title: Variations in the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram

Now that I'm getting into the occult, I'm reassessing a lot of old experiences I had in my agnostic phase. One in particular I wrote quite a bit about because I found the entire thing quite strange.

I was in a post secondary program to teach people how to do speech pathology. The program was run by someone was truly horrible, and who would viciously attack one of the students in the program in such a way it wasn't obvious to others this was what she was doing. She was a Roman Catholic, and so I decided I'd pray to ask God to bless her. I grew up Catholic, so I knew how to go about it. I prayed, asking for God to bless her and protect her.

I then had a number of memories pop up in my head, which seemed to centre on how I was helping her: the program was to train people for speech pathology, and didn't have a linguistics requirement(!), and since my undergraduate degree was in that field, and I spent quite some time teaching people in the program linguistics material which made the rest of the program much easier, including the Roman Catholic; I had the idea of organizing a potluck, and although it was only a handful of us, we had a blast; the times I stood up to the coordinator in her defence; among other things. This was then followed by me realizing how many weird coincidences occurred that led me to be there that year.

Despite a more than 30 year age difference, we've hit things off very well and have kept in touch over the years since. I think this was God saying, "Why do you think you're there?", but I'm not sure. Does it sound like that's plausible to you as well?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Follow up with two things:

a) My notes for the experience include something a few days after: the Roman Catholic, who essentially never used the phrase, "Thank God," thinking it too close to using the Lord's name in vain, used it when I said I knew about one of the things the program coordinator mentioned in one of her lectures and said I would happily go over it with her.

b) Also, reviewing my journals, I found another thing which perhaps helps explain this. I was very depressed after my then best friend decided she didn't want anything to do with me and torched a number of my friendships (and to be fair, hers) in the process of ending ours.

I volunteered with kids, and a number of them were going to participate in a Christmas pagent at an Anglican Church. I decided to go: if they invited me, I saw no harm in it, and I knew I needed to get out of the house anyway. The pagent went really well, the kids had a blast seeing me there, and I was agnostic and not atheist and so I decided to try going to the church.

I didn't feel much from the rituals, and eventually I decided to stop since certain foundational assumptions within Christianity would preclude me from being able to be a good Christian, but the sermon the first Sunday I went was exactly what I needed to hear at the time. The priest was talking about losing friends, and how important it is to keep going: they might not physically be there anymore, but family does, there are other friends, and life goes on.

He was talking about someone in the congregation who had died, but I found it deeply meaningful to my situation: life goes on. Other people care, and we owe it to them to keep going, to live.

I wrote down the following: "If this is God's work, then I owe him, and I hope to be able to offer him something in return."

The first of the strange coincidences which sent me into that program began the next day....

comments.title: Fear of Death/Perplexed by Life
comments.content:
Dear JMG & Community,

First: I've been doing the cell salts protocol since April or so and have yet to comment. I must report: A long-lasting case of chronic, recurring heartburn has abated! I used to take Pepcid on more days than not. No longer! I even ate some very spicy Chinese dishes lately with impunity. Thank you for the protocol! I will continue it.

I've looked into many religious and spiritual paradigms throughout my life, seeking a way to make meaning of life as a whole and my life individually. I've been spending a lot of time looking into nondual approaches in the last several years after having looked into occultism and magic, though with little success despite sincere efforts at self-inquiry. I've read hundreds of near-death accounts. I started out as a Christian and spent a lot of time in my early life in that stream. I've looked into parapsychology and Spiritualism. "I've been everywhere, man."

Beyond the above, I've even had a handful of spiritual experiences with my first one occurring in early childhood. That experience, in so many words, was a voice that urged me to "figure it out" so that I could be "done" and came with a feeling of deep recognition that this was my destiny and purpose in this life.

I've sought long and hard and have done my best to think deeply about life and its purpose and my place in all of this. I haven't managed to do this without getting distracted at times or without having lost my way, but I've been sincere in my effort and I've persisted at least as much as I've thrown up my hands. Nonetheless, I have few convictions. I don't know what I'm doing. I don't feel a strong connection to very much, spiritually. I have no spirit guides that I know of. No Holy Guardian Angel to converse with or to know. No omens or synchronicities inspire my decisions. I don't feel any presence or sense of anything or anyone looking out for me (though I did when younger).

I fear death. I fear illness. I fear what might come after death and I fear what might yet come in this life. Fear is a constant companion. I worry for my family and friends, I worry for the country and the world - I even worry for my cat! Fear drains me of energy and I despise myself for being afraid, but I have little as a means to dispel it.

Is there some decisive step - some simple, straightforward action that someone like me can take to turn the tide when fear and fatigue and weariness have such a hold?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Fear of Death/Perplexed by Life
replies.content: This is the dark night of the soul. The only way to pass through it, and out the other side, is to accept that that's where you are, and to keep on searching.

replies.title: Re: Banishing/Invoking Ritual Design
replies.content: I reached the point of being able to design new banishing rituals after practicing several existing rituals daily for well over twenty years. That kind of background is essential; you have to know from long experience how banishing rituals work and what makes them work, so that you then have something to test your new ritual against. Not all banishing rituals have the kind of numerical structure found in the LBRP -- there are various ways to structure such a ritual.
